title: Python
purpose: Write expressive programs, automate work, build APIs and prepare for data and backend interviews.
outcomes: Explain Python concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important Python features
Implement, test and debug practical Python solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

topics.title: Names, numbers, strings and booleans
topics.definition: A type describes the values an expression may hold and the operations permitted on them. A variable or binding gives a value a name within a scope.
topics.whyItMatters: Types and bindings make state explicit, prevent invalid operations and let readers or tools reason about how data changes.
whenToUse: Represent domain facts with the narrowest useful type
Use immutable bindings for values that should not change
Validate data when it crosses from an untrusted external source
whenNotToUse: Using a broad string or any type for every domain value
Introducing mutable shared state when a value can be passed explicitly
Relying on implicit coercion where correctness matters
internalWorking: A declaration creates a binding in a scope
Primitive values are represented directly while object variables usually hold references
The runtime resolves the binding, evaluates the expression and applies conversion rules
Static languages reject incompatible operations before execution; dynamic languages check during execution
tradeoffs: Narrow types improve safety but require deliberate modeling
Mutation can simplify local algorithms but complicates shared reasoning
Implicit conversion is concise but can hide defects
commonMistakes: Comparing values using identity when content equality is required
Ignoring numeric overflow or floating-point precision
Confusing an uninitialized value with an intentionally empty value
topics.practicalExample: Model an assessment score as a bounded number rather than a free-form string, reject values outside 0-100, and derive eligibility instead of storing a second mutable flag.

topics.title: Conditions, loops and functions
topics.definition: Object-oriented programming groups state with the behavior that protects and transforms it. Encapsulation protects invariants, polymorphism lets callers depend on contracts, and inheritance is one possible reuse mechanism.
topics.whyItMatters: It helps model long-lived domain concepts whose state must remain valid while implementations evolve behind stable interfaces.
whenToUse: Model entities such as Student, Assessment and Submission with clear invariants
Hide changeable implementation details behind an interface
Use polymorphism when several implementations honor the same behavioral contract
whenNotToUse: Creating classes that only hold unrelated utility functions
Using inheritance only to reuse code
Building deep hierarchies where composition expresses the relationship more clearly
internalWorking: An object has identity, state and behavior exposed through methods
A class defines construction and method dispatch rules
Dynamic dispatch selects an overriding implementation using the runtime object type
Encapsulation keeps invalid updates behind methods that enforce invariants
tradeoffs: Stable boundaries versus extra abstractions
Inheritance offers substitution but tightly couples parent and child
Mutable objects are convenient but increase coordination and testing cost
commonMistakes: Calling fields private without actually protecting invariants
Confusing overloading with overriding
Using instanceof chains instead of polymorphic behavior
topics.practicalExample: Assessment.submit(answer) can reject submissions after the deadline. The caller cannot directly mutate the internal status and bypass that rule.

whenToUse: Reject invalid state at the boundary where it is detected
Catch an error only when you can recover, add context or translate it
Preserve the original cause for debugging
whenNotToUse: Catching every exception and continuing
Using exceptions for expected everyday branching
Returning internal stack traces or sensitive details to users
internalWorking: An error interrupts the normal call path and propagates through stack frames
Handlers are searched from the current frame outward
Cleanup must run through finally, defer or a context manager
Application boundaries translate internal failures into stable API or UI error contracts
tradeoffs: Exceptions keep happy paths clean but make control flow non-local
Result types make failures explicit but require propagation code
Detailed context aids debugging but must not leak secrets
commonMistakes: Swallowing the error
Logging and rethrowing at every layer
Catching a type broader than the recovery policy requires
topics.practicalExample: A submission service rejects a closed assessment with a domain error, while the HTTP boundary translates it to a stable 409 response and logs the correlation ID.

topics.title: Asyncio and concurrent execution
topics.definition: Concurrency coordinates multiple tasks whose lifetimes overlap. Parallelism executes work simultaneously; asynchronous I/O lets a task yield while waiting for external work.
topics.whyItMatters: Applications must remain responsive while handling slow networks, files, user requests and CPU work without corrupting shared state.
whenToUse: Use async I/O for many independent network or disk waits
Use bounded worker pools for controlled parallel work
Protect shared mutable state with ownership, immutability or synchronization
whenNotToUse: Adding threads to CPU work without measuring cores and overhead
Blocking an event-loop thread
Holding a lock while performing slow external I/O
internalWorking: A scheduler chooses which runnable task gets CPU time
Async runtimes register I/O readiness and resume continuations later
Threads have independent stacks but share process memory
Synchronization establishes ordering and visibility for shared state
tradeoffs: More throughput versus scheduling and coordination overhead
Shared memory is fast but increases race risk
Async code scales I/O waits but can make cancellation and error propagation harder
commonMistakes: Confusing concurrency with parallelism
Launching unbounded work
Assuming a single statement is automatically thread-safe
topics.practicalExample: Fetch independent question sets concurrently with a timeout, but limit concurrency so one user cannot exhaust database or network connections.

topics.title: Descriptors, metaclasses and data model
topics.definition: Object-oriented programming groups state with the behavior that protects and transforms it. Encapsulation protects invariants, polymorphism lets callers depend on contracts, and inheritance is one possible reuse mechanism.
topics.whyItMatters: It helps model long-lived domain concepts whose state must remain valid while implementations evolve behind stable interfaces.
whenToUse: Model entities such as Student, Assessment and Submission with clear invariants
Hide changeable implementation details behind an interface
Use polymorphism when several implementations honor the same behavioral contract
whenNotToUse: Creating classes that only hold unrelated utility functions
Using inheritance only to reuse code
Building deep hierarchies where composition expresses the relationship more clearly
internalWorking: An object has identity, state and behavior exposed through methods
A class defines construction and method dispatch rules
Dynamic dispatch selects an overriding implementation using the runtime object type
Encapsulation keeps invalid updates behind methods that enforce invariants
tradeoffs: Stable boundaries versus extra abstractions
Inheritance offers substitution but tightly couples parent and child
Mutable objects are convenient but increase coordination and testing cost
commonMistakes: Calling fields private without actually protecting invariants
Confusing overloading with overriding
Using instanceof chains instead of polymorphic behavior
topics.practicalExample: Assessment.submit(answer) can reject submissions after the deadline. The caller cannot directly mutate the internal status and bypass that rule.

topics.title: Dependency isolation and configuration
topics.definition: A transaction groups database operations into one logical unit with atomicity, consistency, isolation and durability guarantees.
topics.whyItMatters: Concurrent requests and failures must not expose half-finished updates or violate business invariants.
whenToUse: Multiple writes must succeed or fail together
Protect invariants such as one submission per attempt
Coordinate reads and writes that require a stable view
whenNotToUse: Holding a transaction open across network calls or user input
Using the strongest isolation without understanding contention
Assuming transactions automatically span unrelated services
internalWorking: The engine records changes in a log before durable data pages
Locks or MVCC control which versions concurrent transactions can observe
Commit makes the unit durable and visible according to isolation
Rollback discards uncommitted effects
tradeoffs: Stronger isolation prevents more anomalies but may block or abort more work
Long transactions preserve a view but retain locks or old versions
Distributed transactions offer coordination at high availability and latency cost
commonMistakes: Doing HTTP calls inside a transaction
Not retrying serialization or deadlock victims
Reading then writing without protecting the invariant
topics.practicalExample: Create an assessment attempt and reserve its unique student-assessment key in one transaction, then publish a notification only after commit.

topics.title: Structured logging and error handling
topics.definition: Error handling represents failure separately from successful results so callers can recover, translate or terminate deliberately.
topics.whyItMatters: Failures cross function and service boundaries. A consistent policy prevents silent corruption and gives users or operators actionable context.
whenToUse: Reject invalid state at the boundary where it is detected
Catch an error only when you can recover, add context or translate it
Preserve the original cause for debugging
whenNotToUse: Catching every exception and continuing
Using exceptions for expected everyday branching
Returning internal stack traces or sensitive details to users
internalWorking: An error interrupts the normal call path and propagates through stack frames
Handlers are searched from the current frame outward
Cleanup must run through finally, defer or a context manager
Application boundaries translate internal failures into stable API or UI error contracts
tradeoffs: Exceptions keep happy paths clean but make control flow non-local
Result types make failures explicit but require propagation code
Detailed context aids debugging but must not leak secrets
commonMistakes: Swallowing the error
Logging and rethrowing at every layer
Catching a type broader than the recovery policy requires
topics.practicalExample: A submission service rejects a closed assessment with a domain error, while the HTTP boundary translates it to a stable 409 response and logs the correlation ID.

topics.title: API validation and database sessions
topics.definition: Authentication proves who a principal is; authorization decides what that principal may do. Sessions, signed tokens and delegated identity protocols carry that decision across requests.
topics.whyItMatters: A system must establish trust without exposing credentials or allowing one user to act outside granted permissions.
whenToUse: Server sessions for revocable browser login with centralized state
Short-lived access tokens for distributed API calls
OAuth/OIDC for delegated access and external identity
whenNotToUse: Putting secrets or sensitive personal data in a JWT payload
Using a long-lived token without rotation or revocation policy
Checking only that a user is logged in without authorizing the resource
internalWorking: Credentials are verified against a slow salted password hash or identity provider
The server creates a session identifier or signs a token
Each request verifies the credential, expiry and intended audience
Authorization evaluates role, attributes and resource ownership before domain work
tradeoffs: Sessions simplify revocation but require shared state
Self-contained tokens reduce lookup but make immediate revocation harder
Fine-grained permissions improve safety but require governance
commonMistakes: Storing browser tokens in a place exposed to XSS
Treating JWT encoding as encryption
Skipping CSRF protection for cookie-authenticated state changes
topics.practicalExample: An organization coordinator may view students only in assigned sections; the API checks both the authenticated identity and section ownership on every request.

interviewQuestions.question: How does API validation and database sessions work internally?

interviewQuestions.question: What are the main tradeoffs of API validation and database sessions?
interviewQuestions.answer: Sessions simplify revocation but require shared state Self-contained tokens reduce lookup but make immediate revocation harder Fine-grained permissions improve safety but require governance

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of API validation and database sessions.

topics.title: Packaging, linting, testing and deployment
topics.definition: Kubernetes is a declarative control system that continuously reconciles cluster resources toward a desired state. Pods run containers; deployments manage replicated stateless pods; services provide stable discovery.
topics.whyItMatters: It automates scheduling, replacement, rollout and connectivity for containerized workloads across multiple machines.
whenToUse: Operate many containerized services with repeatable deployment
Need self-healing, scaling and controlled rollouts
Standardize workload and policy management across teams
whenNotToUse: A small application where operational complexity outweighs value
Stateful software without understanding storage and failure behavior
Using orchestration to hide an unreliable application
internalWorking: Controllers watch desired objects through the API server
The scheduler assigns unscheduled pods to suitable nodes
Node agents start containers and report status
Services and cluster networking route traffic to ready pod endpoints
tradeoffs: Automation and portability versus significant platform complexity
Declarative reconciliation improves recovery but eventual convergence takes time
Resource abstraction helps teams but can obscure infrastructure cost
commonMistakes: Making liveness probes depend on fragile downstream services
Omitting resource requests
Treating a pod IP as stable
topics.practicalExample: Deploy the assessment API with resource requests, a readiness probe that checks local readiness, a liveness probe for deadlock recovery and a rolling update with a disruption budget.
